name: Escape Velocity Calculator
description: Calculate the escape velocity required to overcome a body's gravity.
formulaDescription: Escape velocity is the speed needed to break free from a body's gravitational field without further propulsion.

example.explanation: Earth's escape velocity at the surface is about 11.2 km/s.

faqs.question: What's an escape velocity?
faqs.answer: Minimum speed to break free from gravitational pull: 11.2 km/s for Earth, 5.0 km/s for Mars, 2.4 km/s for Moon.

faqs.question: What's the difference between orbital and escape velocity?
faqs.answer: Orbital: circular path around body. Escape: leaves gravitational field entirely. Escape is √2× orbital velocity.

faqs.question: Can satellites fall from orbit?
faqs.answer: Eventually, yes. Atmospheric drag (low LEO) removes energy slowly; at ~ 120 km they fall in days/weeks.

faqs.question: What's a Hohmann transfer?
faqs.answer: Most fuel-efficient orbital transfer between two circular orbits. Uses elliptical intermediate orbit between them.

faqs.question: Why is GEO orbit special?
faqs.answer: 23-hour 56-minute period matches Earth's rotation. Satellite stays above same point; perfect for communications/weather.

faqs.question: Can I have any altitude for any purpose?
faqs.answer: No. LEO for imaging/ISS, MEO for GPS, GEO for comm/weather. Each has trade-offs in coverage and latency.

concept: Orbital mechanics calculators help analyze satellite orbits, escape velocity, and mission parameters for space missions and orbital mechanics.
guideSteps: Enter orbital parameters (altitude, eccentricity, semi-major axis).
Input celestial body data (mass, radius, gravitational parameter).
Specify mission type (LEO, GEO, escape trajectory, etc.).
Click Calculate for orbital velocity, period, escape velocity, and energy.
Use for satellite mission planning, transfer orbit calculations, and analysis.
standards: Low Earth Orbit (LEO): 200-2,000 km altitude, orbital period 88-127 minutes.
Escape velocity Earth: 11.2 km/s; Mars: 5.0 km/s; Moon: 2.4 km/s.
Orbital velocity: Decreases with altitude (7.8 km/s at LEO, 3.1 km/s at GEO).
Transfer orbits: Hohmann transfer is most fuel-efficient for circular orbits.
